title: Privacy and Memorization
concept: Large language models can memorize sensitive personal data (credit cards, emails) present in public training sets and output them to users, violating privacy.

exercises.prompt: What is a major risk when AI models are trained on massive public datasets?

options.text: The AI learning how to spell words

exercises.explain: Privacy is a major concern because AI models can memorize and reveal personal details they read during training.

cards.title: Do AIs Have Secrets?
cards.body: When AI models read the internet to learn how to write, they sometimes memorize things too well. This means they can accidentally reveal private secrets, like emails or credit card numbers, to total strangers.

cards.title: What is Memorization?
cards.body: Memorization happens when an AI model remembers specific, exact pieces of text from its training data—like private emails, addresses, or phone numbers—and spits them out to users later.

cards.title: The Photographic Student
cards.body: Imagine an AI model as a student with a photographic memory. If they happen to glance at a private diary left open in a public library, they might accidentally repeat those secrets word-for-word to anyone who asks them a question later.

cards.title: An Accidental Leak
cards.body: A user asks an AI to write a sample email template. Instead of generating a fake one, the AI prints out a real, private email address and home phone number belonging to a real person because it memorized it from a web forum.

cards.title: Myth: AI Knows What is Private
cards.body: It might seem like the AI is smart enough to hide private details, but it doesn't actually know what is secret and what is public. If it saw it in the training data, it might repeat it.

cards.title: Safe Generation vs. Memorization
cards.body: Comparing how an AI handles generic information versus memorized private data.

left.text: Generates a fictional phone number like 555-0199 for a creative story.

right.text: Outputs a real person's actual cell phone number found in its training data.

exercises.prompt: Why might a large language model output someone's private email address?

options.text: They only memorize facts about history.
They can memorize personal details present in public training sets.
They are programmed to steal your passwords actively.
They never remember anything they read.

exercises.explain: Because AI models learn by looking at web data, any personal information that was publicly visible online might be memorized and repeated.

exercises.prompt: True or False: AI models are smart enough to automatically filter out all private addresses and phone numbers without special training.
exercises.explain: AI models do not automatically know what is private; they just predict text based on what they have seen, which can include sensitive details.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about AI training risks.
exercises.sentence: When an AI model is trained on web data, it might accidentally ________ sensitive details like phone numbers.
exercises.explain: AI models can sometimes memorize exact lines of text, leading to accidental leaks of sensitive personal information.

exercises.prompt: Which AI response shows a dangerous case of training data memorization?

options.text: "To contact us, please send an email to support@example.com or call 555-0100."
"The owner of the account is John Doe, living at 742 Evergreen Terrace, reachable at 555-4321."
"Please fill out your name and address in the boxes provided on the form."

exercises.explain: The second text shows the AI outputting a real, specific address and phone number, which is a classic sign of memorizing private data. The first text uses placeholder information.

exercises.prompt: Why is training a large language model on public internet data a major risk for individual privacy?

options.text: It can memorize and output private data like emails or credit cards that it saw during training.
It stores passwords in a secure database and shares them only with administrators.
It has a built-in search engine that searches your private files in real-time.
It only remembers information if it is typed in all capital letters.

exercises.explain: Since LLMs are trained on massive amounts of web text, they can accidentally memorize and reproduce private details like emails or phone numbers that were scraped during training.
